claims.claim: We investigate whether a cached in-memory vector index can reduce query-time retrieval latency for a dense/category-aware retriever on the CUAD contract-understanding benchmark without degrading retrieval hit rate or downstream answer retention.

evidence_refs.quote: # CUAD Dense Retriever Productionization with Cached Vector Index

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: In a 240-question benchmark over native long contracts, the cached index reduced p95 query retrieval latency from 126.7 ms to 28.7 ms (4.42×) and p50 latency from 39.5 ms to 15.2 ms (2.60×), while preserving retrieval hit (0.779) and entity-deduplicated answer retention at all tested context budgets exactly.

evidence_refs.quote: # CUAD Dense Retriever Productionization with Cached Vector Index

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: The cached dense/category retriever also maintained its quality advantage over a lexical baseline (hit 0.688, 1024-token retention 0.533).

claims.claim: However, the query-time cost of computing dense vectors for every contract window at retrieval time yielded p50 latency of 39.6 ms and p95 latency of 126.6 ms, which may be prohibitive in latency-sensitive serving settings.

claims.claim: This work tests whether precomputing and caching per-contract window vectors, token sets, and length normalizers in an in-memory index can substantially reduce query-time latency while preserving retrieval quality.

evidence_refs.quote: # CUAD Dense Retriever Productionization with Cached Vector Index

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: We define a strict productionization threshold: the cached path must reduce p95 latency by at least 2× while keeping retrieval hit within 1 percentage point and entity-deduplicated answer retention within 1 percentage point of the uncached dense/category retriever.

evidence_refs.quote: # CUAD Dense Retriever Productionization with Cached Vector Index

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: The implementation adds three components to the existing dense/category retriever: - **`IndexedChunk`**: A data structure holding a precomputed dense vector, a hashed representation of that vector, a token set for lexical overlap computation, and a length normalizer for each contract window.

evidence_refs.quote: # CUAD Dense Retriever Productionization with Cached Vector Index

claims.claim: `retrieve_dense_category_cached`**: A retrieval function that, at query time, looks up precomputed chunks from the index rather than recomputing vectors, token sets, and normalizers.
